    16|---
    17|
    18|# Lien Resolution Delays Settlement Disbursement
    19|
    20|> Even after a settlement is reached and accepted, disbursement to the client can be delayed by weeks or months due to unresolved liens. Medicaid liens are particularly problematic because the state actively monitors and may increase lien amounts during negotiations. Third-party lien resolution services (ZipLiens) are used to manage complex lien negotiations but cannot guarantee timely resolution.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|The connection between lien resolution and disbursement delay operates as follows:
    25|
    26|1. **Settlement Accepted**: Client agrees to the settlement amount
    27|2. **Lien Resolution Begins**: Firm must obtain final lien amounts from all lien holders before calculating net to client
    28|3. **Delay Factors**:
    29|   - Medicaid lien holders may increase their claimed amounts during reduction negotiations
    30|   - Health insurance lien holders may request copies of the settlement release before completing negotiations
    31|   - Out-of-state Medicaid (e.g., Ohio Medicaid) may require portal-based submissions instead of email
    32|   - Multiple liens must all be resolved before disbursement
    33|4. **Client Frustration**: Client has been told the case is settled but cannot receive their check
    34|
    35|## Evidence
    36|
    37|- In one case, Ohio Medicaid lien negotiations were managed through ZipLiens. The Medicaid program "shifted gears" and required everything previously communicated by email to be resubmitted through their portal. During reduction negotiations, Medicaid responded with a "drastically increased" lien amount rather than the expected reduction, requiring the firm to go back and verify the itemization before continuing negotiations.
    38|- In the same case, Medicaid requested a copy of the settlement release from the liability carrier before continuing lien negotiations, adding another dependency. The firm was told "it's unlikely they'll continue the negotiations until they receive it."
    39|- In an MVA case with Conduent (KY Medicaid), the lien was coordinated with PIP payments and KAC claims simultaneously, requiring the firm to track three separate payment sources against one lien.
    40|- In a case with both BI settlement and Medicaid lien, the carrier required "direct payment of any medicare/Medicaid liens and then a separate check being made to your office for the remainder" — splitting the settlement payment into multiple checks.
    41|- In 5+ cases, clients called the firm asking when they could pick up their settlement check, only to be told the check had not yet been received from the carrier or that lien resolution was still pending.
    42|- In one case, a client called months after receiving the settlement check complaining about bills still going to collections, indicating that lien resolution had either not covered all bills or had not been communicated to the client.
    43|
    44|## Agent Guidance
    45|
    46|1. Begin lien resolution immediately upon settlement acceptance — do not wait for the carrier check
    47|2. Use ZipLiens or similar services for complex lien negotiations, especially Medicaid
    48|3. Set client expectations that disbursement may take 2-6 weeks after settlement due to lien resolution
    49|4. Track all lien holders and their response status in a single dashboard
    50|5. When Medicaid increases lien amounts during negotiations, verify the itemization with the attorney before proceeding
    51|
    52|## Related
    53|
    54|- [[settlement-disbursement-workflow]] — the disbursement that lien resolution delays
    55|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — private health insurance lien process
    56|- [[medicaid-lien-management]] — Medicaid liens are the most common delay cause
    57|- [[pre-settlement-funding-obligations]] — funding payoffs are an additional disbursement dependency
    58|", "total_lines": 57, "file_size": 4161, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Negotiation After Demand
     3|slug: negotiation-after-demand
     4|phase: [phase_4_negotiation]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [negotiation, settlement, offer, counter, client-rejection, demand-followup]
     7|confidence: high
     8|evidence_count: 146
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|
    12|related:
    13|  - demand-preparation-workflow
    14|  - client-expectations-management
    15|  - attorney-review-triggers
    16|  - statute-of-limitations-monitoring
    17|---
    18|
    19|# Negotiation After Demand
    20|
    21|> After a demand is sent, the case enters negotiation with the insurance adjuster. This phase involves waiting for and evaluating the initial offer, communicating with the client about the offer, potential counter-offers, and either settlement or escalation to litigation. The negotiation phase can be complicated by client dissatisfaction, offer rejection, and lien resolution.
    22|
    23|## Pattern
    24|
    25|### Post-Demand Sequence
    26|1. **Demand Sent**: Follow-up task created for adjuster response
    27|2. **Adjuster Response**: Initial offer received (or adjuster requests more time/information)
    28|3. **Attorney Evaluation**: Attorney evaluates the offer against case value, medical specials, liens
    29|4. **Client Communication**: Client informed of offer and attorney recommendation
    30|5. **Decision Point**: Accept, counter-offer, or reject and prepare for litigation
    31|
    32|### Client Rejection Pattern
    33|A significant pattern emerges when clients reject settlement offers:
    34|- The case may be labeled "Settlement" prematurely (before client accepts)
    35|- Client calls in asking for an update, is told the case is "on settlement"
    36|- Client explicitly rejects the offer ("I am not settling")
    37|- The case must be re-classified from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation"
    38|- This back-and-forth creates frustration for all parties
    39|
    40|### Adjuster Follow-Up
    41|- Adjusters may need to be prompted for a response after demand
    42|- The firm sends follow-up communications to the adjuster
    43|- Lien amounts must be resolved during this phase to calculate the client's net recovery
    44|
    45|### Communication Challenges
    46|- Clients in negotiation become increasingly frustrated with the pace
    47|- Missed calls and voicemails escalate in urgency and frustration
    48|- Clients may threaten to "take another route" (change attorneys)
    49|- Regular, proactive communication is critical during this phase
    50|
    51|## Evidence
    52|
    53|- In an auto accident case, the demand was sent and the case moved to "Settlement" phase. However, the client explicitly refused the offer — a voicemail stated "I told you I am not settling for what you are... so come back." The phase had to be changed from "Settlement" back to "Negotiation." Staff confusion was evident: one team member noted "appears was settled but client didn't accept so maybe ongoing negotiations."
    54|- In the same case, the client's frustration escalated over months: text messages included "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today" (6/2024) and multiple missed calls with voicemails demanding updates (3/2025, 8/2025, 10/2025, 2/2026, 3/2026). The attorney requested the adjuster pay a lien from Rawlings (10/2025), indicating lien resolution was happening during negotiation.
    55|- The case demonstrated a pattern where the firm told the client the case was "on settlement phase" and "we can't disclose the amount yet" — which created client frustration when the settlement did not materialize quickly.
    56|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2024, KY, negotiation phase), one of two BI carriers (a state mutual) settled quickly after demand, while the second carrier (a regional insurer) remained in active negotiation. The adjuster from the second carrier returned a voicemail indicating willingness to discuss the claim. This demonstrates a split-settlement pattern: when multiple BI carriers are involved, individual carriers may settle at different times, creating a phased resolution. The SOL countdown (180 days noted in an automated alert) remained active on the open claim, adding urgency to the second negotiation track.
    57|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY), the case bypassed the demand phase and moved directly from treatment to litigation due to disputed liability. During litigation, defense counsel offered to "negotiate 50/50." The attorney discussed this with the client, explaining the case issues and defense counsel's proposal. This confirms that in disputed-liability cases, negotiation may occur within the litigation context rather than following the standard demand-offer-counter sequence.
    58|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff), the negotiation phase had a unique twist: the BI carrier tendered policy limits (25/50) within one day of the demand being sent, but settlement could not proceed because the limits had to be split among four injured plaintiffs represented by different firms. The negotiation shifted from firm-vs-insurer to inter-counsel allocation. The attorney emailed all co-plaintiffs' counsel to negotiate distribution. One attorney was slow to provide medical bills, stalling the process for months. The firm updated the client regularly but could only say "we are still waiting." When inter-counsel agreement was not reached, the case moved to litigation. This shows a novel negotiation pattern: the insurer has already paid limits, but the money cannot be distributed without all parties' agreement.
    59|- In Case R (auto accident, minor injury — airbag rash, DOI 4/2023), a $5,000 demand was sent to the BI carrier. The carrier responded with a $500 "nuisance offer." The firm refused to negotiate further until the defendant had been deposed. After the defendant's deposition (3/2025 — nearly 2 years after the accident), the negotiation was expected to resume. This demonstrates a pattern where the firm deliberately pauses negotiation to build evidentiary leverage through litigation discovery, rather than accepting lowball offers on minor injury cases.
    60|- In Case S (auto accident, government transit defendant, DOI 9/2021), a BI settlement offer of $15,000 was received from a government risk management entity during active litigation. The firm was simultaneously corresponding with the government entity's attorney about the case. The negotiation during litigation pattern — where offers continue even after complaint filing — is confirmed by this case. Additionally, multiple liens (Medicare, Humana at $1,513, and health insurance) had to be resolved in parallel with the negotiation, complicating the settlement math on a government-defendant case.
    61|- In 4 cases from this batch (DOIs mid-2025 to late-2025, KY), demand was sent via fax/email to BI adjusters with attached records/bills, and the paralegal followed up requesting offer status. In one case, the firm demanded "policy limits" and attached a narrative report as supplemental support. The adjuster responded but the offer details were not yet visible. In another, both sides denied liability (rental vehicle driver), with the paralegal reporting to the attorney "both sides are denying liability on this. Not sure how to proceed" — demonstrating that disputed liability can stall negotiation entirely. In a third case, the firm sent demand to the BI adjuster and then followed up 2 weeks later asking to "confirm received" — the follow-up cadence of 2 weeks post-demand is a consistent pattern.
    62|- In a negotiation-phase case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client was discovered to be incarcerated (in county jail) during active negotiation.
    63|- In 4 additional small MVA cases (KY, DOIs 6/2025-9/2025, negotiation phase), post-demand patterns confirmed: (1) In one case, the BI adjuster reviewed the PIP log after receiving the demand and found discrepancies: "what PIP has paid is significantly higher than the records from the demand show" — the adjuster requested additional records before making an offer. The firm provided the records and the adjuster responded with "increased offers for your clients" (companion case with coordinated offers). This confirms that adjusters use PIP payment data as a cross-check against demand records, and discrepancies delay offers. (2) In a multi-carrier case (2 BI carriers + 1 PIP), the firm sent demands to each BI carrier independently and followed up 3 days later requesting receipt confirmation. One carrier (Progressive) had not responded after 2 follow-ups over 2 weeks. (3) In one case, the demand was sent to National General BI via fax and email. No response was documented in the activity logs over 5+ months post-demand — confirming the "black hole" pattern where some carriers simply do not acknowledge demands. (4) In a companion case pair, the demand follow-up was "Demand Sent - Follow up with BI Adjuster" — a standardized Filevine task auto-generated upon demand dispatch.
    64|
    65|## Agent Guidance
    66|
    67|1. After demand is sent, do NOT prematurely label the case as "Settlement" until the client has agreed to accept an offer
    68|2. When an offer is received, schedule a call with the client to discuss — do not communicate offers via text
    69|3. If the client rejects an offer, document the rejection clearly and move the case back to "Negotiation"
    70|4. Maintain regular contact during negotiation (at minimum every 2 weeks)
    71|5. When the client asks for updates, provide specific information about what is happening and what's next
    72|6. During negotiation, resolve liens in parallel — don't wait until after settlement to address them
    73|7. If the client threatens to leave, escalate to the attorney immediately for a personal call
    74|8. Track every client contact attempt (successful or missed) in the activity log
    75|
    76|## Pitfalls
    77|
    78|- Premature phase change to "Settlement" before client agreement
    79|- Telling the client the case is in "settlement" when it's actually in negotiation (creates false expectations)
    80|- Not communicating offer details to the client promptly
    81|- Allowing weeks/months of silence during active negotiation
    82|
    83|### Batch 2 Evidence (30 additional cases)
    84|
    85|- In 29 cases from this batch, the standard demand-to-offer sequence was observed: demand sent, 2-week follow-up, initial offer received. Initial offers ranged from very low ($250 on a minor case, $1,500 on a soft-tissue case) to near-policy-limits ($41K, $100K policy tender). The typical time from demand to initial offer was 2-4 weeks when adjusters were responsive.
    86|- In a soft-tissue case (DOI 3/2024), the initial BI offer was $1,500. The firm obtained client authority to negotiate but "not pull trigger" without final approval. A new adjuster was assigned mid-negotiation, requiring file re-review. The adjuster ultimately offered $2,500-$3,000 range. The client was "iffy" on $3,000 but needed rental reimbursement resolved first — showing how ancillary PD/rental issues can delay BI settlement.
    87|- In a case (DOI 4/2022), the carrier's demand response included specific defense arguments: "can't place any value on the future medical expenses... she has not treated in almost two years... already treating for her knee injury when the accident occurred." The offer was "exclusive of PIP" with conditions: indemnity/hold harmless, no admission, confidentiality, complete release. This demonstrates sophisticated adjuster evaluation on pre-existing condition cases.
    88|- In a case (DOI 6/2024), after a counter of $10,500, the carrier responded: "It appears we are very far apart... extending our top and final offer of $4,000, exclusive of PIP, inclusive of all other liens." This "top and final" language is a common carrier tactic to shut down further negotiation.
    89|- "LBF" (Last Best and Final) is used extensively internally — the firm obtains client "authority to negotiate to LBF" before engaging. In 6+ cases, the pattern was: receive offer, contact client for authority, negotiate to LBF, present final number for client acceptance.
    90|- In 2 cases, the adjuster proactively called to follow up on whether the client had accepted the offer — showing adjusters track response timing and may push for closure.
    91|- In a case involving prior counsel (DOI 6/2022), the firm had to obtain PIP ledger urgently to "confirm SOL" before proceeding, adding SOL verification as a prerequisite to negotiation when taking over cases.
    92|- Automated Filevine task "Demand Sent - Follow up with BI Adjuster" was observed in 10+ cases, confirming standardized post-demand workflow.
    93|- In 3 cases, the attorney personally called the adjuster to negotiate after the paralegal's initial contact, confirming attorney involvement is triggered when initial offers are far below case value.
    94|- Staff not knowing the current case status when client calls in
    95|- Not resolving liens during negotiation (delays final settlement)
    96|- Client losing trust due to poor communication (may terminate representation)
    97|
    98|### Batch 3 Evidence (15 additional cases from batch 1)
    99|
   100|- In a litigated MVA case (DOI 8/2019, KY, Nelson Circuit Court), a complete negotiation sequence was documented: demand sent 3/2024 -> defense counsel responded with $6,000 offer (inclusive of liens excl. PIP, with hold harmless/indemnity + agreed order of dismissal) -> firm countered at $20,000 -> defense raised to $6,500 -> firm countered at $13,000 -> defense raised to $7,000 -> attorney predicted they could reach $10,000 -> defense LBF at $7,500. Client rejected the LBF, citing ongoing neck issues and the 5+ year duration. This case shows the classic narrowing-gap pattern: defense starts at 30% of demand and slowly moves up, while the firm reduces demand by roughly 35% each round.
   101|- In a companion MVA case (DOI 4/2024, KY), the negotiation was resolved through attorney-to-adjuster phone call: initial offer $3,000, settled at $4,500. The attorney obtained authority from the client's POA holder (client was nonverbal/disabled). This confirms that for cases with POA representatives, the authority-to-settle process must go through the legal representative.
   102|- In a UM claim (DOI 9/2023, KY), the adjuster made an initial offer of $4,350 but was missing billing. After resending bills, the firm made repeated "calm" (called and left message) attempts over 3 months (18+ call attempts logged). LBF was $8,000. This case demonstrates extreme adjuster delay in responding — the firm logged "calm" nearly daily for weeks without response.
   103|- In an MVA case (DOI 11/2024, KY), the adjuster emailed a detailed counter: "$7,500 counter demand... We are able to extend an offer of $6,031... You advised that if we can get to $7,000 we can get this done." This shows adjusters documenting the entire phone negotiation in follow-up emails — creating a paper trail of verbal commitments.
   104|- In an MVA case (DOI 6/2023, KY), a prior demand existed at $15K and the carrier had an existing offer. The firm sent an EvenUp-generated demand and received a $21,670.50 initial offer. Client authorized "pull trigger at LBF." Settled at $40,000. This EvenUp demand achieved roughly 2x the initial offer, suggesting AI-generated demands may produce higher initial offers from carriers.
   105|- In a case (DOI 11/2023, KY), the firm received an initial offer of $40,502 on $100K policy limits. Settled at $50,000. The settlement at 50% of policy limits after initial offer at 40% shows limited negotiation headroom on higher-limit policies.
   106|- In an MVA case (DOI 8/2024, KY), the firm sent demand and received an initial offer of $4,000 within one month. Client was unreachable for weeks (phone not working, texts wouldn't go through, emails sent). Client eventually accepted at $4,900 "top offer." This confirms that client unreachability during negotiation can result in accepting lower offers due to inability to coordinate counter-strategy.
   107|- In a multi-track case (DOI 1/2023, KY), the PIP adjuster (Shelter Insurance) called the firm repeatedly (5+ voicemails over months) asking whether to close the PIP file, whether a PIP demand would be sent, and requesting an updated Medicare lien. The firm did not return calls for extended periods. The adjuster escalated to threatening file closure. This is a pattern where firm non-responsiveness to PIP adjusters creates risk of losing PIP recovery.
   108|- Settlement values observed in this batch: $4,500 (companion case), $7,000 (settled at mediation), $9,500 (MVA), $10,000 (WC), $15,000 (MVA with med pay for lien), $40,000 (EvenUp demand), $50,000 (high-limit case). The range confirms that Kentucky PI settlements for standard MVA soft-tissue cases cluster between $4,000-$15,000, with higher values requiring extensive treatment or EvenUp demands.
   109|- In 7+ cases, the "settled inclusive" vs "settled exclusive of PIP/med pay" distinction was critical: carriers specified whether the settlement amount included or excluded PIP/med pay obligations. The default in Kentucky appears to be "exclusive of PIP" (settlement is new money on top of PIP payments), but some releases include language about PIP subrogation indemnity that creates post-settlement complications.
   110|- In 4 cases, the release signing process was itself a bottleneck: clients failed to sign releases promptly (one case had multiple vinesign resend attempts), and in one case the BI carrier had not received the signed release months after settlement, delaying PIP reimbursement to the carrier. The adjuster called repeatedly requesting the signed release.
   111|
   112|### Batch 3 Evidence (25 additional cases)
   113|
   114|- In 25+ cases from this batch, detailed negotiation sequences were documented with specific offer amounts. The typical negotiation pattern: demand sent -> initial offer at 30-50% of demand -> firm counters -> 1-3 rounds of back-and-forth -> settlement at 50-70% of original demand.
   115|- In a case (KY, BI claim), detailed negotiation notes showed: initial offer $22K -> firm countered at $35K -> carrier raised to $25K -> settled at $27K. This 3-round negotiation pattern is the most commonly observed sequence.
   116|- In a KY Farm Bureau BI case, the initial offer was $35,500 on what appears to be a higher-value case. The adjuster requested additional records (PCP records from Valley View Health) before continuing evaluation. The firm sent updated records, and the adjuster confirmed receipt — but the follow-up cadence extended to months rather than weeks when additional records were involved.
   117|- In a case with Allstate BI, the demand produced an initial offer of $9,000. The firm noted "2/20 calm" (called and left message) — the "calm" abbreviation for adjuster follow-up calls is used consistently across 10+ cases in this batch.
   118|- In a case involving a prior accident (client had a 2nd MVA after the subject accident), the adjuster refused to consider chiropractic treatment, saying they would only value "DOL treatment only" at $3,500. The adjuster cited the subsequent accident as the basis for excluding the chiropractic. This confirms that subsequent accidents are used as a defense to limit treatment valuation in the same way that prior accidents are.
   119|- In a KY MVA case, the demand was sent and the adjuster tendered policy limits ($25K) within 1-2 days, triggering the COOTS letter process for UIM claim. The demand-to-tender timeline of 1-2 days for clear-liability cases with significant treatment is now confirmed across multiple cases.
   120|- In a case with a nuisance offer ($650 on a demand), the client was unreachable (phone disconnected, email bounced back, Lexis search confirmed disconnected numbers). The firm eventually settled at $1,200 — suggesting that unreachable clients force the firm to accept lower offers.
   121|- In a companion case pair (same accident, guardian and minor), both demands were sent to the same UM adjuster. The adjuster made an initial offer of $3,234.95 for the minor and a separate evaluation for the adult. The firm demanded $22,000 for the minor and waited for the adult's demand to go out simultaneously. This batch-demand approach for companions remains consistent.
   122|- In a BI claim that settled, the adjuster sent a formal email: "settlement agreement was for $2,000 exclusive of PIP... I will need the release signed and returned to me prior to making payment." The requirement to return the signed release BEFORE payment is issued is a consistent carrier requirement across 10+ cases.
   123|- In a UIM case (same carrier as PIP), the UIM adjuster offered $21,000 exclusive of PIP after computing that "after PIP payment and tortfeasor payment, he has been compensated around $4,800." The UIM adjuster's math explicitly deducted prior PIP and BI payments from the UIM evaluation, confirming that UIM value is calculated as incremental over prior recovery.
   124|- In a case with pre-settlement funding, the funding company (Barrister Capital) proactively contacted the firm asking about the case status and whether "there is acceptance from at fault" and "Is PIP paying?" — demonstrating that funding companies actively monitor cases and create additional communication overhead.
   125|- In 5+ cases, the attorney obtained "authority to pull trigger" from the client before accepting offers. This language is used consistently to confirm client authorization for settlement acceptance.
   126|
   127|## Related
   128|
   129|- [[demand-preparation-workflow]] — the demand that precedes negotiation
   130|- [[client-expectations-management]] — managing client expectations during the long negotiation phase
   131|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — all offers require attorney evaluation
   132|- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — SOL may force a decision during negotiation
   133|
   134|### Batch 4 Evidence (55 settlement activity cases)
   135|
   136|- In 55 cases from this batch, the standard post-demand negotiation sequence was observed across multiple carriers (Progressive, State Farm, Allstate, Liberty Mutual, USAA, Canal, Safeco, Erie, Shelter). Settlement values ranged from $2,150 (minor soft-tissue case) to $38,000+ (multi-provider treatment with significant medicals).
   137|- In a premises liability case (DOI 8/2023, KY), a detailed counter-offer exchange was documented: initial offer $9,600 -> firm countered at $25,000 -> carrier raised to $7,600 -> firm countered at $15,000 -> settled at $15,000. This 4-round negotiation pattern confirms the narrowing-gap convergence at 50-70% of demand.
   138|- In a high-value MVA case (DOI 1/2024, KY), the initial offer was $29,000 from Kentucky Farm Bureau. The firm logged "2/20 calm" (called and left message) and subsequent notes showed the offer increased to $38,000. The CALM abbreviation for adjuster follow-up calls continues to be used consistently.
   139|- In a UM case (DOI 11/2022, KY), the negotiation involved escalation to the UM adjuster's supervisor when the initial adjuster was unresponsive. A new UM adjuster was then assigned with "demand review in progress." The authority pattern confirmed: the firm noted getting "authority at $5K but I think I can push the adjuster up a little bit."
   140|- In a soft-tissue MVA case (DOI 7/2022, KY), the negotiation was simple: initial offer received, the firm asked "If you can get to $2,500" and it settled at $2,150. This demonstrates that low-value cases have minimal negotiation rounds.
   141|- In a multi-plaintiff MVA case (DOI 10/2021, KY), the negotiation was complicated by multiple parties represented by different firms: the BI carrier had to coordinate with co-plaintiff counsel for split agreement. The client's paralegal noted "your claim is taking a bit longer due to the number of parties involved."
   142|- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2021, KY), a SOL near-miss was documented: the attorney emailed staff "You have to pay attention to these. We missed the Statute of Limitations" — the demand was sent as a "quick demand with what's available" to preserve the claim. The adjuster applied Robinson v. Bates to reduce medical specials, and the case settled quickly at $5,000.
   143|- In 36 cases, settlement checks were issued, confirming the pipeline from offer acceptance through release signing to check issuance. VineSign was used for release signing in 29 cases.
   144|- In companion case pairs (DOI 2/2024, 7/2023), offers were made for both companions simultaneously by the same adjuster, with separate settlement amounts. One adjuster offered $4,250 for both adult and minor companion.
   145|- In 4+ cases, the adjuster's formal settlement email included specific conditions: "exclusive of PIP subrogation claim," "inclusive of all other claims including Medicare/Medicaid," "hold harmless/indemnity." The "exclusive of PIP" language remains the standard in Kentucky settlements.
   146|
   147|### Batch 5 Evidence (8 supplementary cases)
   148|
   149|- In a multi-plaintiff MVA case (DOI 6/2021, KY, minor child with facial scar), a detailed multi-round negotiation was documented between attorney and Indiana Farm Bureau adjuster. The adjuster's initial offer was $2,500 pain-and-suffering for the minor's soft tissue claim, plus offered to pay medical ($3,703.22) through med pay. The firm countered at $75,000 (citing facial scar on a child). The adjuster replied that she had "room to move, but not to limits" and requested a counter demand. This case demonstrates that scar/disfigurement claims on minors — especially facial scars — are valued dramatically higher by the firm than standard soft-tissue claims, and adjusters expect counter-demands rather than "remaining limits" as a blanket response.
   150|- In a case (DOI 3/2021, KY), the adjuster (American Family) called back during settlement to flag a child support lien discovered in the file, asking about its status. This confirms that adjusters independently identify child support liens during settlement processing, which can complicate disbursement.
   151|- In a multi-party MVA case (DOI 2/2022, KY, litigation), co-plaintiff counsel (separate firm) and the at-fault carrier (Progressive) agreed to mediate the split of a $50K policy among multiple plaintiffs. The adjuster "offered to pay to mediate." The firm's attorney questioned whether the case should simply be resolved with the at-fault carrier rather than pursuing UIM against Allstate (which was dismissed). This shows a novel pattern: the at-fault carrier offering to fund mediation for inter-plaintiff allocation disputes.
   152|", "total_lines": 151, "file_size": 25920, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

Good. Cases with >50 logs need sampling. Let me now read the activity logs systematically. I'll use a script to batch-read them.

function.name: terminal
function.arguments:
{"command": "cd /opt/data/FirmVault && for slug in will-bryant-mva-9-20-21 amelia-jones-mva-9-8-2023 kenita-triplett-mva-5-5-2024 pedrea-boggs-premise-7-30-2024 yvonne-mcilwain-mva-12-9-2023; do
  echo "========== $slug =========="
  dir="cases/$slug/Activity Log"
  count=$(ls "$dir" | wc -l)
  if [ "$count" -gt 50 ]; then
    files=($(ls -t "$dir"))
    # First 10 and last 10
    selected=("${files[@]:0:10}" "${files[@]:$((count-10)):10}")
    for f in "${selected[@]}"; do echo "--- $f ---"; head -60 "$dir/$f"; echo "..."; done
  else
    for f in $(ls -t "$dir"); do echo "--- $f ---"; head -60 "$dir/$f"; echo "..."; done
  fi
done 2>&1 | head -3000", "timeout": 60}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Client Frustration Escalation Pattern
     3|slug: client-frustration-escalation
     4|phase: [phase_3_demand, phase_4_negotiation, phase_5_settlement]
     5|category: communication

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    14|  - attorney-review-triggers
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern
    18|
    19|> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|The escalation follows a predictable trajectory:
    24|
    25|### Stage 1: Polite Inquiry
    26|- Client texts or calls asking for a general update
    27|- "I would like to know where we are on this case"
    28|- "Just checking in"
    29|
    30|### Stage 2: Urgent Demand
    31|- Client's tone shifts to urgency
    32|- "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    33|- Multiple calls in a single day (3+ calls on same day observed)
    34|- Voicemails become longer and more frustrated
    35|
    36|### Stage 3: Threats and Distrust
    37|- Client explicitly threatens to leave
    38|- "I am not settling for what you are"
    39|- "You keep in touch with me or nothing"
    40|- "If you will today, you just please call me back today and just give me something"
    41|- Client may call multiple staff members when the attorney is unavailable
    42|
    43|### Stage 4: Repeated Unanswered Attempts
    44|- Client calls are logged as "missed calls" repeatedly over weeks/months
    45|- Voicemail transcriptions become shorter and more resigned
    46|- Client may call different staff members (attorney, paralegal, receptionist)
    47|
    48|### Key Triggers
    49|- Long periods of no proactive contact from the firm
    50|- Settlement offer communicated without full explanation
    51|- Being told the case is "on settlement" without a timeline
    52|- Calls going unreturned or to voicemail
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In an auto accident case (negotiation phase), the client's frustration escalated over 12+ months:
    57|  - 6/2024: "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    58|  - 3/2025: Voicemail — "will you please get back in contact with me... you keep in touch with me or nothing... call me back today and just give me something. I told you I am not settling"
    59|  - 3/2025-3/2026: Multiple missed calls logged (3/21/2025 alone had 2 missed calls + 1 outbound call + texts)
    60|  - 8/2025: Multiple missed calls to different staff members
    61|  - 2/2026, 3/2026: Continued missed calls with empty voicemail transcriptions
    62|- In a premises liability case, the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out" — followed by the attorney providing a substantive update about next steps.
    63|- In Case H (MVA, air evac, 2.5+ year case), the client communicated through a go-between (Ann Dattillo). Client called for updates repeatedly — "said she had not heard from anyone recently." Client was confused about deposition process ("said that her attorney was to come out to her residence"). After attorney departure, client needed reassurance about new point of contact. The intermediary requested a copy of the deposition and case update. This shows Stage 2-3 frustration compounded by staff turnover.
    64|- In Case I (MVA, disputed liability, litigation), the client's calls went unreturned for extended periods — 4 missed calls logged between Feb-Mar 2026 with no callback documented. Client had previously been responsive and cooperative (provided provider info via text, completed deposition). The communication breakdown occurred post-deposition when the case was in a waiting period. This confirms the pattern that frustration peaks during litigation gaps.
    65|- In a UM/litigation MVA case (DOI 1/2023), a family member texted: "Hi, checking with you to see if there has been any settlement agreement yet." Two months later another text: "We haven't heard a single thing about [the] case. What's going on with it? It's been well long enough." Then: "Hello, we haven't heard anything at all about my case. After the deposition, you said things went well and we had to wait a couple weeks before an agreement/settlement." The post-deposition waiting period triggered escalating frustration from both the client and a family proxy.
    66|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client called the firm repeatedly over Oct-Feb (10+ missed calls across months). Calls were to the managing attorney but never answered. The client texted "Good Afternoon Mr. [attorney]" and "Is your office open today" — showing politeness that may mask frustration. The reverse pattern (firm unreachable to client) is equally damaging.
    67|- In a multi-carrier MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client (who initially reported cancelled medical appointments and transportation hardship within days of intake) continued calling the firm repeatedly — 10+ missed calls logged over Jan-Mar 2026. The firm made periodic outbound calls, but the volume of unreturned client calls suggests significant frustration.
    68|- In a WC+BI case (DOI 6/2021), the client called multiple times in Dec 2025 — 3 missed calls in a single day to different staff members (attorney, paralegal). The firm eventually made outbound calls. The "shotgun dialing" pattern (calling every staff member sequentially) is a Stage 3 indicator.
    69|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), the client had multiple missed calls across Oct-Dec 2025. The firm was in the records-gathering phase and had not yet sent the demand. SOL countdown warnings (90 days, 180 days) were firing while the client was unable to reach the firm for updates.
    70|- In Case H, client communication was relayed through "Anna per Client instructions" — the intermediary called for updates when the client felt out of the loop. After Bryce Koon's departure from the firm, the case was reassigned, and the client needed to be re-introduced to the new attorney — a staff transition that exacerbated the frustration pattern.
    71|- In Case F (auto accident, litigation), the client called multiple staff members repeatedly during active litigation (6/2025-3/2026): missed calls to the associate attorney, then to the lead attorney, then to the paralegal — all on the same day (3/3/2026). The voicemail was forwarded internally, and the attorney returned the call the same day. This demonstrates that even when substantive litigation activity is ongoing (depositions being scheduled), clients still escalate if they are not proactively informed.
    72|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), the client showed Stage 1 behavior at 6/2025 (calling about documents she expected but hadn't received), followed by steady escalation with missed calls to multiple staff members (7/2025, 8/2025 — 3 missed calls in 8 minutes on 8/22/2025), continuing through 12/2025-1/2026. The case had $31K in pre-settlement funding creating additional financial pressure.
    73|- In Case H (auto accident, 4+ year litigation), the client texted "Good morning was reaching out for an update" (3/2025) and "Hello was wondering if we had an update on our case haven't heard anything since March" (5/2025). The firm responded within a day with specific information about deposition scheduling. This shows that proactive updates after court events prevent escalation.
    74|- In a workers' compensation case (workplace injury, treatment phase), the client sent emails with increasingly urgent subject lines: "ANY UPDATES?" (8/2025) and "UPDATES??" (10/2025). The escalating punctuation and capitalization follow the same trajectory as the phone-based pattern. The firm responded to each, but the repeated pattern over months confirms that even cases in active treatment (not just negotiation) can trigger client frustration when the client perceives a lack of forward progress. The client also asked about deposition scheduling details and case status, suggesting the frustration was compounded by not understanding the WC litigation process.
    75|- In Case M (MVA, multi-plaintiff, negotiation/litigation), the client's frustration followed a textbook escalation: (1) polite inquiry phase with regular updates from the firm (Aug-Dec 2023), (2) frustration peaked when the client was told "we are still waiting on [another plaintiff's] medical bills" repeatedly over months (Jan-Apr 2024), (3) the client called to complain about "not receiving call backs or being told conflicting information repeatedly" — she came to the office in person to demand an update (Feb 2024), (4) the attorney spoke with her directly and calmed the situation. The pattern continued in the litigation phase with multiple missed calls (Sep-Nov 2025), text message difficulties ("Can u you give me a call it won't lemme call you"), and the attorney texting back and forth to coordinate. This case uniquely shows frustration driven by inter-counsel coordination delays — the client's case was ready but held hostage by another firm's inaction.
    76|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023, transferred from prior counsel), the client made 20+ missed calls to the attorney over a 7-month period (Aug 2025 - Mar 2026). The attorney returned calls periodically but the ratio was approximately 5:1 client calls to attorney callbacks. The pattern was remarkably persistent — multiple calls per week with no voicemail content logged, suggesting either hang-ups or voicemail not recorded. The client's frustration is compounded by the case being transferred from prior counsel (already frustrated) and the complex litigation (multiple defendants, out-of-state service).
    77|- In a dog bite case involving a minor (DOI 8/2022), the guardian (mother) made regular missed calls to both the associate and lead attorney. After the assigned associate attorney changed (case handoff to new attorney), missed calls increased. The guardian called the lead attorney directly when the associate was unresponsive. Depositions were cancelled and rescheduled multiple times by defense counsel, adding to guardian frustration about case progress. Despite positive deposition outcome ("went very well"), the communication gap continued.
    78|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client called repeatedly (missed calls to both paralegal and attorney) but the client's own voicemail was full, preventing the firm from leaving messages. The communication breakdown was bidirectional — neither party could reach the other effectively. The firm noted "voicemail full" when trying to call back.
    79|- In a WC case (DOI 5/2023), the client had regular communication through treatment monitoring, but the case shifted dramatically when the firm learned of the client's death via the client's mother's voicemail. This extreme case demonstrates that communication patterns can change instantly and family members may be the first to notify the firm of critical events.
    80|- In Case N (premises liability, estate case, 5+ year litigation), the surviving spouse called for updates repeatedly throughout 2025-2026. The pattern showed: July 2025, the spouse called saying he "wants to speak with attorney" and "has not rec'd a return call." The firm provided an update via the paralegal. Missed calls continued through Sep 2025, Oct 2025 (two missed calls same day), Dec 2025 (two more missed calls), and Mar 2026. This demonstrates that estate cases, where the primary contact is a surviving family member rather than the injured party, follow the same frustration escalation. The emotional dimension is heightened — the spouse is dealing with both grief and the legal process. The 5+ year timeline makes sustained communication even more critical.
    81|- In Case T (premises liability, DOI 9/2023), the client made repeated missed calls to the attorney over a concentrated period (Feb-Mar 2026) — 7+ missed calls in approximately 6 weeks. The calls came in pairs (back-to-back within minutes), suggesting the client was calling, not getting through, and immediately redialing. The case was in active negotiation with defense counsel during this period, confirming that clients escalate even when the case IS moving — the issue is the client doesn't know it's moving.
    82|- In Case U (auto accident, employer liability/taxi company, DOI 9/2024), the client made an extraordinary number of missed calls — 8+ in the last 6 weeks of the activity log alone (Jan-Mar 2026), including back-to-back calls within minutes. The case involved bifurcated litigation issues and a Medicare lien, adding complexity. The client's frustration is notable because the case was relatively recent (DOI just 17 months prior), suggesting that litigation complexity and Medicare bureaucracy trigger faster frustration escalation even on newer cases.
    83|- In Case V (auto accident, closed case, DOI 12/2024), the client made multiple missed calls in September 2025 and continued calling even after the settlement phase. The client expressed frustration about still receiving medical bills despite the case progressing, asking the firm to help with bill payment. The firm told the client to pay bills and submit receipts for reimbursement from PIP. This shows a unique frustration trigger: clients who are being contacted by bill collectors while their case is in settlement/disbursement — the frustration is not about case progress but about the tangible financial pain of unpaid bills.
    84|- In a small MVA case (DOI 8/2025, negotiation phase), the client left a voicemail for the managing attorney after approximately 8 months of case activity, stating: "I have had a case going on with [paralegal] for several months now, like 8 months. I have not been able to get a hold of them for pretty much the past 2 months and I am trying to see what is going on with my case or at least get a call back from somebody." This confirms the Stage 2-3 escalation pattern: client bypasses the assigned paralegal and calls the attorney directly when the paralegal is unresponsive. The 2-month communication gap is consistent with the typical frustration trigger window.
    85|- In a recent MVA case (DOI 2/2026, treatment/demand phase), the client left a voicemail: "I have been trying to call you and get in contact with you or somebody and I can not get through. If you could call me when you get a chance... I would greatly appreciate it." The voicemail transcription showed the client attempted to reach the firm by calling both the main number and the managing attorney directly. This occurred less than 2 months after intake, demonstrating that client frustration can begin very early in the case lifecycle — not just during the demand/negotiation phase.
    86|
    87|## Agent Guidance
    88|
    89|1. Set automated 2-week check-in reminders for every active case in demand/negotiation phase
    90|2. When a client calls and you cannot provide an update, say WHEN they can expect one (e.g., "I will have an update by Friday")
    91|3. If you cannot reach the client (their voicemail is full or not set up), send a text immediately
    92|4. When a client shows Stage 2 behavior, escalate to the attorney for a personal call within 24 hours
    93|5. Never tell a client the case is "on settlement" unless the client has already agreed to the amount
    94|6. After every client contact, set the next follow-up date in the system
    95|7. Document all contact attempts — even missed calls — in the activity log
    96|8. When multiple missed calls from one client occur in a day, treat it as an urgent response needed
    97|
    98|## Pitfalls
    99|
   100|- Letting client calls go unreturned for days/weeks
   101|- Client voicemail being full (preventing you from leaving a message) without pivoting to text
   102|- Staff saying "I'm not sure on status" when client calls — everyone on the case should know the current status
   103|- Responding to frustration with vague reassurances instead of specific next steps
   104|- Not escalating frustrated clients to the attorney
   105|- Allowing the pattern to reach Stage 4 before taking action
   106|
   107|### Batch 2 Evidence (20 additional cases)
   108|
   109|- In 20 cases from this batch, client frustration patterns were observed. The most common trigger was delayed communication during the negotiation phase — clients wanting to know "what's going on with my case" after weeks/months of silence.
   110|- In a case (DOI 3/2024), the client explicitly complained about the 40% attorney fee and said "she's looking for a different attorney." When no one responded to this concern promptly, the client repeated the complaint 2 weeks later. The fee dispute layered on top of communication frustration nearly resulted in client attrition.
   111|- In a case (DOI 7/2023), the client's settlement check dispute demonstrated post-settlement frustration: the client "states her agreement was that she was to receive $30K in her pocket" but the check was for $24,900 at 40% attorney fee. The client stated "that is not the settlement statement that she signed." Post-settlement fee misunderstandings are a distinct frustration trigger.
   112|- In 5 cases, the "two-week follow up" task fired 95 times total across the batch, confirming the firm's automated check-in cadence. However, the sheer volume of follow-up tasks suggests some are being auto-generated without substantive contact occurring.
   113|- In a negotiation case, the client couldn't be reached through any channel: "no voicemail, texts don't go through, tried email which got kicked back because their email is full" — demonstrating a case where the firm wanted to communicate but literally could not reach the client.
   114|- In 3 cases, the insurance adjuster contacted the client directly (bypassing the firm), which created confusion and frustration. In one case, the adjuster told the client "we no longer repped her" despite the firm having declined the case — the client called upset because she didn't know about the decline.
   115|
   116|## Related
   117|
   118|- [[client-expectations-management]] — proactive management prevents this escalation
   119|- [[negotiation-after-demand]] — negotiation phase is when frustration peaks
   120|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — frustrated clients should be escalated to attorney
   121|", "total_lines": 120, "file_size": 18261, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Unreachable Client Patterns
     3|slug: unreachable-client-patterns
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment]
     5|category: communication
     6|tags: [client-communication, unreachable, voicemail-full, no-contact, phone-tag, text-fallback]
     7|confidence: high

     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-frustration-escalation
    13|  - client-expectations-management
    14|  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
    15|  - case-decline-and-closing-workflow
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Unreachable Client Patterns
    19|
    20|> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Common Unreachability Signals
    25|1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
    26|2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
    27|3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
    28|4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered
    29|
    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023) in active litigation, the client called the attorney repeatedly (20+ missed calls over 7 months), but the calls went unreturned for extended periods. The attorney then made periodic outbound calls. This is a reverse unreachability pattern — the firm is unreachable to the client rather than vice versa, likely due to attorney bandwidth in a litigation-heavy caseload.
    57|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 4/2023, KY) in active litigation, the firm attempted to call the client multiple times but the phone "just rings and then gives busy signal." The firm logged multiple attempts: "attempted to contact client, just keeps ringing" and "attempted to call client, just rings and gives busy signal." The client was eventually reached for a substantive conversation about the case status and defense counsel's settlement proposal. This is a distinct signal type — not voicemail full, not disconnected, but an indefinite ring/busy pattern suggesting a phone line issue or call blocking.
    59|- In an auto accident case (DOI 1/2024, KY) during a scheduled Zoom deposition, the firm had to text the client urgently: "Dana you good? We lost you" and "They were having some technical problems. Go ahead and log back on." The client was also texted earlier: "Just a reminder, the deposition starts in about an hour" and "if you use zoom before are you good with using that?" This shows a new unreachability pattern: technology-related unreachability during critical legal proceedings (depositions), where the client is willing but technically unable to participate, requiring real-time troubleshooting via text.
    60|- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client's phone number was "not in service" when the firm tried to reach her during intake. A text was sent and a second number was tried. The client was eventually reached via an alternate number. This was a pattern of phone service disruption rather than willful non-response.
    61|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client called the firm repeatedly but no one answered — calls were logged as "missed" to multiple staff members. The client also texted: "Is your office open today?" The firm's unreachability to the client (many missed inbound calls over months) is the reverse pattern, creating friction and frustration.
    62|- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client was initially unreachable — "call went to VM - left msg." The client then used an alternate phone number for subsequent contact. New phone numbers should always be captured immediately.
    63|- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the client preferred text over phone calls. When staff called, the client was unavailable ("on an errand"). Text became the primary channel. Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops.
    64|- In a demand-phase case (DOI 12/2025), the client's voicemail was full when the firm tried to call with a case update. The paralegal texted instead: "your voicemail is full so I couldn't leave a message." Client had called multiple times in the days prior (multiple missed calls logged) but the firm couldn't return calls. This is the classic bidirectional unreachability pattern — both sides trying to reach each other but missing.
    65|- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 8/2025), the client called multiple times (missed calls logged weeks apart) but received no callbacks. The case was in demand preparation but the client had no updates. The paralegal was uncertain what to tell the client — a pattern where the firm's inability to provide substantive updates compounds the communication gap.
    66|- In an MVA case (DOI 9/2025) with disputed liability, both insurance carriers were denying fault. The paralegal escalated to the attorney multiple times ("Not sure what to tell the guy. Any ideas?") but received no response documented in the logs. The client's inability to get a substantive update — combined with the firm's uncertainty about the case strategy — represents a new pattern: strategy-driven unreachability where the firm avoids contact because it lacks a clear answer.
    67|- In a file-setup MVA case (DOI 9/2024, KY), the initial contact attempt to the client went to voicemail which was full. The paralegal noted "Went straight to vm. VM is full." The client's boyfriend (a companion/passenger in the same accident) had initially called in. Multiple attempts were needed before the client could be reached for a welcome call. The pattern of VM-full on day one of file setup was confirmed in 3 additional cases.
    68|- In an MVA case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client called the firm's main line (missed calls were logged to the attorney) but the firm did not return calls promptly. The missed call logs showed repeated attempts weeks apart. This reverse unreachability pattern — where the client is available but the firm is not — was confirmed across 4+ cases in this batch, particularly with smaller or early-stage cases that may not receive priority callback attention.
    69|- In a case (DOI mid-2025, KY), the client was described as "difficult" and repeatedly asked to meet with the attorney in person. The paralegal communicated to the attorney: "[Client] is difficult. Nice guy but keeps asking to meet with you." This is a distinct communication pattern — not unreachability per se, but a client whose communication needs exceed the standard phone/text model, requiring in-person attorney engagement.
    70|
    71|## Agent Guidance
    72|
    73|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message
    74|2. If no response to text, send an email the same day
    75|3. Set a 2-week follow-up task automatically on every case where the client has not responded
    76|4. Log EVERY contact attempt (call, text, email) with outcome (answered, VM full, no answer, etc.)
    77|5. After 3 failed contact attempts, escalate to the attorney with a note about unreachability
    78|6. Always ask the client for a secondary contact number and an emergency contact
    79|7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
    80|8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
    81|9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
    82|10. When reaching out to a new client, provide the paralegal's direct phone number via text so the client can easily call back: "It's [name] from the firm. My direct number is [number]" — confirmed across multiple cases as an effective first-contact strategy
    83|
    84|## Pitfalls
    85|
    86|- Only trying to call and not using text/email as fallback
    87|- Not logging failed contact attempts (creates the appearance of no effort)
    88|- Waiting weeks between contact attempts instead of escalating channels
    89|- Not asking for a secondary contact number at intake
    90|- Not updating the phone number in the system when the client calls from a new number
    91|- Assuming unreachability means the client is uninterested — they may be dealing with medical/life issues
    92|
    93|## Related
    94|
    95|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — opposite pattern where the client is trying to reach the firm
    96|- [[client-expectations-management]] — unreachable clients may still have expectations about case progress
    97|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — unreachability most critically impacts intake tasks
    98|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — persistent unreachability may contribute to case decline decisions
    99|
   100|### Batch 3 Evidence (8 additional cases)
   101|
   102|- In 8+ cases from this batch, clients were unreachable during critical negotiation phases. Patterns observed:
   103|  - Phone numbers disconnected or changed without notifying the firm (2 cases)
   104|  - Text messages would not go through (1 case)
   105|  - Multiple VM + text + email attempts over 2+ weeks with no response (3 cases)
   106|  - Client eventually called back after sending a contact letter (1 case)
   107|  - Client's phone number "was no good" — required updating contact card when client finally called (1 case)
   108|- In an MVA case (DOI 8/2024, KY), the client was unreachable for 3+ weeks during active negotiation. The firm attempted calls, texts, and emails. The client's phone number was not working and texts would not go through. An email was sent as a last resort. The client eventually responded and accepted the $4,900 offer — but the unreachability prevented counter-negotiation that might have improved the outcome.
   109|- In a case (DOI 1/2024, KY), the attorney personally called the client: "My office has been trying to reach you. Give me a call or let me know a good time to call so we can talk about your case and get it settled. Put some money in your pocket." This attorney-level outreach was used after paralegal-level attempts failed, confirming escalation protocol.
   110|- The standard escalation for unreachable clients in this batch was: (1) call + VM, (2) text message, (3) email, (4) contact letter by mail, (5) attorney personal call. The full escalation took 2-4 weeks before the attorney was involved.
   111|", "total_lines": 110, "file_size": 12872, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    15|---
    16|
    17|# Pre-Settlement Funding Obligations
    18|
    19|> Some PI clients obtain "pre-settlement funding" (lawsuit loans) from third-party lenders while their case is pending. These loans create obligations that must be satisfied from the settlement proceeds, reducing the client's net recovery. The firm must understand its obligation to the funder and factor the loan into settlement math.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Pre-settlement funding creates a complex financial picture at settlement:
    24|
    25|1. Client takes a loan from a funding company (e.g., "Claims Angel") during the pendency of the case
    26|2. The loan accrues interest and fees over time
    27|3. At settlement, the funding must be repaid from the client's share of proceeds
    28|4. The attorney must factor the loan payoff into the disbursement sheet
    29|
    30|Settlement math with pre-settlement funding:
    31|- Gross settlement
    32|- Less: Attorney fees
    33|- Less: Case costs
    34|- Less: Medical provider payments/liens
    35|- Less: Pre-settlement funding payoff
    36|- Equals: Client's net recovery
    37|
    38|When the settlement offer is modest, pre-settlement funding can consume a large portion of what's left, leaving the client with very little.
    39|
    40|## Evidence
    41|
    42|- In Case A, the client had "loans" from a company named "Claims Angel" totaling $6,537.05. With an $18K offer, after paying the loan ($6,537.05) and 50% of the medical provider bill ($5,397.50), and attorney fees, the client would receive less than $3K. The paralegal noted: "I'm not sure what obligation we have to satisfy the presettlement funding."
    43|- In Case D (long-running litigation/negotiation), a loan contract was sent to the client in May 2025 — approximately 2.5 years after the accident. The funding occurred during active litigation while awaiting IMEs and depositions, illustrating that funding pressure increases the longer a case takes to resolve.
    44|- In Case G (auto accident, UIM litigation), a $31,200 lien from Legal Funding Partners was identified. The case involved extensive treatment ($45K+ hospital bills alone) and a UIM claim, suggesting the client sought funding during the prolonged treatment/litigation period. The high funding amount relative to case value demonstrates the risk of pre-settlement funding in complex cases — it can consume a large portion of the recovery.
    45|- In Case G, the client's expenses included $5,000 listed as a payment to the client, plus $500 for EvenUP and $495 for Digital Owl (case evaluation tools), illustrating the range of costs that accumulate during complex litigation beyond just the funding itself.
    46|
    47|## Agent Guidance
    48|
    49|1. During intake, ask if the client has obtained any pre-settlement funding or lawsuit loans
    50|2. If yes, request the funding agreement and current payoff amount
    51|3. Log the funder name, original loan amount, and current payoff in the case financial records
    52|4. Factor the funding payoff into all settlement evaluations
    53|5. When presenting settlement options to the client, show the complete disbursement breakdown including the funding payoff
    54|6. Ask the attorney about the firm's legal obligation to satisfy the funding (varies by jurisdiction and agreement)
    55|7. If the settlement barely covers obligations, flag this to the attorney early
    56|
    57|## Pitfalls
    58|
    59|- Not discovering the pre-settlement funding until settlement disbursement
    60|- Not understanding the firm's legal obligation to the funder
    61|- Not factoring funding into the settlement evaluation, leading to unrealistic client expectations
    62|- Assuming the payoff amount is the same as the original loan (interest and fees accrue)
    63|
    64|## Related
    65|
    66|- [[settlement-offer-evaluation]] — funding obligations affect whether an offer is adequate
    67|- [[demand-value-factors]] — knowing the funding obligation influences demand strategy
    68|- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — liens and funding together determine net recovery
    69|", "total_lines": 68, "file_size": 4207, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
